content: It’s natural to be skeptical about black holes—they sound like science fiction. But black holes are real objects predicted by Einstein’s theory of gravity and supported by strong evidence. Simply put, a black hole forms when a massive star collapses, packing so much mass into a small space that its gravity becomes incredibly intense. To escape an object’s gravity, you need to travel faster than its escape velocity—the speed required to break free. For Earth, that’s about 25,000 miles per hour. For a black hole, the escape velocity exceeds the speed of light, which is the fastest anything can go. Since nothing can move faster than light, nothing—not even light itself—can escape once it gets too close. Scientists have observed stars orbiting invisible massive objects and have even captured images of black hole shadows, confirming their existence. So, while they may sound extraordinary, black holes are simply extreme results of well-understood physics.
